questions.question: Case: You run a multi-region AKS microservice. Design a single Azure DevOps YAML pipeline to build a multi-arch image, push to ACR, and promote releases to EastUS, WestEU, and Southeast Asia using blue/green per region with Front Door traffic routing. Include per-tenant feature flags from App Configuration, secrets from Key Vault, policy gates before prod, post-deploy synthetic tests and drift checks via Log Analytics, auto-rollback on failure, and full audit to Azure Monitor. Outline the pipeline and key tasks?
questions.answer: Outline a single YAML pipeline that builds a multi-arch image, pushes to ACR, promotes blue/green releases in EastUS, WestEU, and Southeast Asia via Helm on AKS, with Front Door routing. Pull per-tena
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests orchestration of multi-region, blue/green deployments with real-world constraints: feature flags, secrets, policy gates, post-deploy validation, drift detection, and audit trails at scale. It also probes how candidates structure YAML pipelines, manage regional rollouts, and implement robust rollback. 

## Key Concepts
- Azure DevOps YAML pipelines
- AKS multi-region deployments
- Blue/green release strategy per region
- Front Door traffic routing and per-region rules
- Azure App Configuration per-tenant feature flags
- Azure Key Vault secrets integration
- Policy gates and approvals
- Post-deploy health tests and drift checks via Azure Monitor Logs
- Auto-rollback and persistent audit trails

## Code Example
```yaml
# Minimal scaffold illustrating core stages
trigger:
  - main
pool:
  vmImage: 'ubuntu-latest'
stages:
  - stage: Build
    jobs:
      - job: BuildImage
        steps:
          - script: |
              docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 -t $(ACR)/svc:$(Build.BuildId) --push .
            displayName: 'Build and push multi-arch image'
  - stage: Deploy-EastUS
    dependsOn: Build
    jobs:
      - job: Deploy
        steps:
          - task: HelmInstaller@1
          - script: |
              helm upgrade --install svc-east us-charts/svc -f values-east.yaml --set image.tag=$(Build.BuildId)
            displayName: 'Deploy EastUS blue/green'
  # Additional stages for WestEU and Southeast Asia would mirror Deploy-EastUS
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you parameterize per-tenant App Configuration keys and secrets? 
- What are your rollback criteria thresholds and how would you implement them with Front Door re-routing?

flowchart TD
  A[Build] --> B[Push to ACR]

  C --> D[EastUS: Blue/Green]
  C --> E[WestEU: Blue/Green]
  C --> F[SE Asia: Blue/Green]
  D --> G[Front Door routing rules]
  E --> G
  F --> G
  G --> H[Post-deploy tests & drift checks]
  H --> I{Health OK?}
  I -->|Yes| J[Audit to Azure Monitor]
  I -->|No| K[Auto-rollback to previous green] 
  K --> L[Front Door re-point] 
  L --> M[Retry or fail]
  M --> J

questions.question: Design an Azure DevOps YAML pipeline that, on PR creation, provisions an ephemeral AKS namespace, deploys via ArgoCD from a GitOps repo, uses per-env secrets from Azure Key Vault and per-env feature flags from Azure App Configuration, enforces a cost budget via Azure Cost Management before syncing, runs post-deploy health checks and synthetic tests, and tears down after 48 hours or PR closure, with all events logged to Azure Monitor?
questions.answer: Design an Azure DevOps YAML pipeline that, on PR creation, provisions an ephemeral AKS namespace, deploys via ArgoCD from a GitOps repo, uses per-env secrets from Azure Key Vault and per-env feature flags from Azure App Configuration, enforces a cost budget via Azure Cost Management before syncing, runs post-deploy health checks and synthetic tests, and tears down after 48 hours or PR closure, with all events logged to Azure Monitor.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Assesses concrete integration of GitOps (ArgoCD) inside Azure DevOps pipelines, ephemeral environments, cost governance, secret management, and observability at scale.

## Key Concepts
- GitOps with ArgoCD in Azure DevOps pipelines
- Ephemeral AKS namespaces per PR
- Secrets from Azure Key Vault per environment
- Feature flags via Azure App Configuration
- Azure Cost Management budgets as gate before deploy
- Post-deployment health checks and synthetic tests
- Automatic teardown on PR close or after grace period
- End-to-end logging in Azure Monitor

## Code Example
```yaml
trigger: none
pr:
  branches:
    include:
    - main

variables:
  - group: 'shared-infrastructure'
  - name: kubernetesNamespace
    value: 'pr-$(System.PullRequest.PullRequestId)'
  - name: gitopsRepo
    value: 'https://github.com/contoso/app-config'
  - name: argoApp
    value: 'pr-$(System.PullRequest.PullRequestId)'

stages:
- stage: Provision
  displayName: 'Provision Ephemeral Environment'
  jobs:
  - job: CreateNamespace
    displayName: 'Create AKS Namespace'
    steps:
    - task: AzureCLI@2
      displayName: 'Create Kubernetes Namespace'
      inputs:
        azureSubscription: '$(azureServiceConnection)'
        scriptType: 'bash'
        scriptLocation: 'inlineScript'
        inlineScript: |
          az aks get-credentials --resource-group $(resourceGroup) --name $(aksCluster)
          kubectl create namespace $(kubernetesNamespace) --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -
          kubectl label namespace $(kubernetesNamespace) environment=ephemeral pr-id=$(System.PullRequest.PullRequestId)

- stage: CostGate
  displayName: 'Cost Budget Validation'
  dependsOn: Provision
  condition: succeeded()
  jobs:
  - job: CheckBudget
    displayName: 'Validate Cost Budget'
    steps:
    - task: AzureCLI@2
      displayName: 'Check Cost Management Budget'
      inputs:
        azureSubscription: '$(azureServiceConnection)'
        scriptType: 'bash'
        scriptLocation: 'inlineScript'
        inlineScript: |
          currentSpend=$(az costmanagement query --scope "/subscriptions/$(subscriptionId)" --type "ActualCost" --timeframe "MonthToDate" --query "[0].Amount" -o tsv)
          budgetLimit=$(az consumption budget list --resource-group $(resourceGroup) --query "[0].amount" -o tsv)
          if (( $(echo "$currentSpend >= $budgetLimit" | bc -l) )); then
            echo "Cost budget exceeded: $currentSpend >= $budgetLimit"
            exit 1
          fi
          echo "Cost budget check passed: $currentSpend < $budgetLimit"

- stage: Deploy
  displayName: 'Deploy via ArgoCD'
  dependsOn: CostGate
  condition: succeeded()
  jobs:
  - job: ArgoCDDeploy
    displayName: 'Deploy Application'
    steps:
    - task: AzureCLI@2
      displayName: 'Create ArgoCD Application'
      inputs:
        azureSubscription: '$(azureServiceConnection)'
        scriptType: 'bash'
        scriptLocation: 'inlineScript'
        inlineScript: |
          az aks get-credentials --resource-group $(resourceGroup) --name $(aksCluster)
          cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
          apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
          kind: Application
          metadata:
            name: $(argoApp)
            namespace: argocd
          spec:
            project: ephemeral
            source:
              repoURL: $(gitopsRepo)
              targetRevision: HEAD
              path: environments/$(kubernetesNamespace)
              helm:
                values: |
                  namespace: $(kubernetesNamespace)
                  secrets:
                    keyVaultName: $(keyVaultName)
                    secretNames: ["app-db-connection", "app-api-key"]
                  featureFlags:
                    appConfigurationName: $(appConfigurationName)
                    filter: "env=$(kubernetesNamespace)"
            destination:
              server: https://kubernetes.default.svc
              namespace: $(kubernetesNamespace)
            syncPolicy:
              automated:
                prune: true
                selfHeal: true
              syncOptions:
                - CreateNamespace=false
                - PrunePropagationPolicy=foreground
          EOF

    - task: AzureCLI@2
      displayName: 'Wait for ArgoCD Sync'
      inputs:
        azureSubscription: '$(azureServiceConnection)'
        scriptType: 'bash'
        scriptLocation: 'inlineScript'
        inlineScript: |
          az aks get-credentials --resource-group $(resourceGroup) --name $(aksCluster)
          timeout=600
          interval=10
          elapsed=0
          while [ $elapsed -lt $timeout ]; do
            status=$(kubectl get application $(argoApp) -n argocd -o jsonpath='{.status.health.status}')
            if [ "$status" = "Healthy" ]; then
              echo "Application deployed successfully"
              break
            fi
            echo "Waiting for deployment... ($elapsed/$timeout seconds)"
            sleep $interval
            elapsed=$((elapsed + interval))
          done
          if [ $elapsed -ge $timeout ]; then
            echo "Deployment timeout reached"
            exit 1
          fi

- stage: Validate
  displayName: 'Post-Deployment Validation'
  dependsOn: Deploy
  condition: succeeded()
  jobs:
  - job: HealthChecks
    displayName: 'Run Health Checks'
    steps:
    - task: Bash@3
      displayName: 'Application Health Check'
      inputs:
        targetType: 'inline'
        script: |
          echo "Running health checks for $(kubernetesNamespace)..."
          # Add your health check commands here
          # Example: curl -f http://service-url/health

    - job: SyntheticTests
      displayName: 'Run Synthetic Tests'
      dependsOn: HealthChecks
      steps:
      - task: Bash@3
        displayName: 'Execute Synthetic Tests'
        inputs:
          targetType: 'inline'
          script: |
            echo "Running synthetic tests..."
            # Add your synthetic test commands here
            # Example: npm run test:synthetic

- stage: Teardown
  displayName: 'Teardown Environment'
  dependsOn: Validate
  condition: always()
  jobs:
  - job: Cleanup
    displayName: 'Clean Resources'
    timeoutInMinutes: 30
    steps:
    - task: AzureCLI@2
      displayName: 'Remove ArgoCD Application'
      inputs:
        azureSubscription: '$(azureServiceConnection)'
        scriptType: 'bash'
        scriptLocation: 'inlineScript'
        inlineScript: |
          az aks get-credentials --resource-group $(resourceGroup) --name $(aksCluster)
          kubectl delete application $(argoApp) -n argocd --ignore-not-found=true

    - task: AzureCLI@2
      displayName: 'Remove Namespace'
      inputs:
        azureSubscription: '$(azureServiceConnection)'
        scriptType: 'bash'
        scriptLocation: 'inlineScript'
        inlineScript: |
          az aks get-credentials --resource-group $(resourceGroup) --name $(aksCluster)
          kubectl delete namespace $(kubernetesNamespace) --ignore-not-found=true
          echo "Namespace $(kubernetesNamespace) cleaned up successfully"

- stage: AutoTeardown
  displayName: 'Scheduled Teardown (48 hours)'
  condition: and(always(), eq(variables['Build.SourceBranch'], 'refs/heads/main'))
  jobs:
  - job: DelayedCleanup
    displayName: 'Wait and Cleanup'
    timeoutInMinutes: 2880  # 48 hours
    steps:
    - task: AzureCLI@2
      displayName: 'Timeout Cleanup'
      inputs:
        azureSubscription: '$(azureServiceConnection)'
        scriptType: 'bash'
        scriptLocation: 'inlineScript'
        inlineScript: |
          echo "48-hour timeout reached, cleaning up environment"
          az aks get-credentials --resource-group $(resourceGroup) --name $(aksCluster)
          kubectl delete namespace $(kubernetesNamespace) --ignore-not-found=true
          kubectl delete application $(argoApp) -n argocd --ignore-not-found=true
```

## Event Logging to Azure Monitor
```yaml
# Add to each stage
- task: AzureMonitor@0
  displayName: 'Log to Azure Monitor'
  inputs:
    azureSubscription: '$(azureServiceConnection)'
    resourceGroup: '$(monitoringResourceGroup)'
    logType: 'customLog'
    logData: |
      {
        "timestamp": "$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%Z)",
        "stage": "$(Agent.JobName)",
        "pullRequest": "$(System.PullRequest.PullRequestId)",
        "namespace": "$(kubernetesNamespace)",
        "status": "$(Agent.JobStatus)",
        "buildId": "$(Build.BuildId)"
      }
```

questions.question: Design a beginner Azure DevOps YAML pipeline that builds a Node.js API from Azure Repos, runs tests, builds a Docker image, pushes to Azure Container Registry, deploys to an Azure App Service (Linux) using a staging slot, performs a health check, and if healthy, swaps to production; if not, rollback. Include per-environment feature flags from Azure App Configuration and log steps to Log Analytics. What would the YAML look like?
questions.answer: CI runs npm test, builds and pushes a Docker image to ACR, deploys to App Service staging, reads per-environment flags from App Configuration, runs a health check; if healthy, swap to production; if n
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests a realistic, beginner-friendly pipeline that covers core Azure DevOps tasks: CI, Docker, ACR, App Service slots, App Configuration integration, health-based swap, and Log Analytics auditing.

## Key Concepts

- YAML pipelines and multi-stage design
- Docker build/push to Azure Container Registry
- Azure App Service deployment slots (staging/production) and slot swap
- Azure App Configuration for per-environment flags
- Health checks and rollback strategies
- Logging/auditing to Azure Monitor / Log Analytics
- Service connections and least-privilege access

## Code Example

```yaml
trigger:
- main
pool:
  vmImage: ubuntu-latest
variables:
  imageName: myapi
  registry: <ACR_NAME>.azurecr.io
stages:
- stage: Build
  jobs:
  - job: Test
    steps:
    - task: NodeTool@0
      inputs: {version: '18.x'}
    - script: npm install
    - script: npm test
- stage: Deploy
  jobs:
  - job: Deploy
    steps:
    - task: Docker@2
      inputs:
        command: buildAndPush
        containerRegistry: '<registry-service-connection>'
        repository: '$(registry)/$(imageName)'
        Dockerfile: '**/Dockerfile'
        tags: |
          $(Build.BuildId)
    - task: AzureWebAppContainer@1
      inputs:
        azureSubscription: '<subscription-connection>'
        appName: '<app-service-name>'
        imageName: '$(registry)/$(imageName):$(Build.BuildId)'
        resourceGroup: '<rg>'
        slotName: 'staging'
    - task: AzureCLI@2
      inputs:
        azureSubscription: '<subscription-connection>'
        scriptType: 'bash'
        script: |
          HEALTH_URL=https://<app-service-name>-staging.azurewebsites.net/health
          if curl -sSf $HEALTH_URL | grep -q 'ok'; then
            az webapp deployment slot swap --resource-group <rg> --name <app-service-name> --slot staging --target-slot production
          else
            az webapp deployment slot swap --resource-group <rg> --name <app-service-name> --slot production --target-slot staging
            exit 1
          fi
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you add a retry policy for the health check without masking failures?
- How would you extend this to support a blue/green rollout for multiple regions?
questions.diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[PR/Commit] --> B[CI: Build & Tests]
  B --> C[Create & Push Docker Image to ACR]
  C --> D[Deploy to App Service - staging]
  D --> E{Health Check}
  E -->|Healthy| F[Slot Swap to Production]
  E -->|Fail| G[Rollback & Alert]

Include a minimal YAML template and gating approach?
questions.answer: Create a multi-stage YAML template that builds per-service artifacts, tags region-scoped versions, and promotes to dev, stage, and prod environments. Gate promotions with environment approvals tied to
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests ability to design scalable cross-region release pipelines with policy gates and PR governance across many services.

## Key Concepts

- Multi-stage YAML pipelines
- Environments with approvals
- Azure Policy checks across subscriptions
- PR linking to Azure Boards
- Template-driven scalability

## Code Example

```javascript
# Minimal region template
parameters:
  - name: service
    type: string
  - name: region
    type: string

trigger:
  branches:
    include:
      - main

stages:
- stage: Build
  jobs:
  - job: Build
    steps:
      - script: echo "Build ${{ parameters.service }}"
        displayName: 'Build artifact'

- stage: Deploy
  dependsOn: Build
  environment: '${{ parameters.region }}'
  strategy:
    runOnce:
      deploy:
        steps:
          - script: echo "Deploy ${{ parameters.service }} to ${{ parameters.region }}"
```

- How would you extend this to automatically rollback on policy violation?

questions.question: Design an Azure DevOps pipeline to upgrade an Azure Redis Cache deployment with canary testing across regions: stage a canary shard upgrade, route 10% of traffic through Front Door to the canary, monitor latency and error rates via Log Analytics, and promote to full production if metrics stay within thresholds for 24 hours; otherwise, rollback to the previous shard and pause traffic. Include per-env secrets from Key Vault, policy approvals, and audit logging?
questions.answer: Implement a two-stage release for Azure Redis upgrade using a canary shard. Pipeline steps: pull per-environment secrets from Key Vault, deploy new Redis version to a canary shard, route 10% traffic v
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to design a production-grade data-plane release with real-world constraints: cross-region deployment, canary traffic splits, observability via Log Analytics, policy gating, and irreversible rollback paths.

## Key Concepts
- Canary deployments with traffic shaping via Front Door
- Data-plane upgrade of a Redis shard and shard retirement
- Per-environment secrets from Azure Key Vault
- Compliance and change approvals via Azure Policy gates
- Post-deploy validation using Azure Monitor / Log Analytics and automated rollback

## Code Example
```yaml
trigger:
  branches:
    include:
      - main
stages:
  - stage: CanaryUpgrade
    jobs:
      - job: DeployCanary
        steps:
          - task: AzureKeyVault@2
            inputs:
              connectedServiceName: 'AZURE_SP'
              keyVaultName: 'kv-${{ parameters.env }}'
              secretsFilter: 'RedisAdminPassword'
          - script: |
              # Create canary shard and apply upgrade
              az redis update --name canary-redis --resource-group rg-${{ parameters.env }} --set version=6.0
          - script: |
              # Route 10% traffic via Front Door
              az front-door routing-rule update --name canary-rule --front-door-name fd-${{ parameters.env }} --set routingRules[0].backendPool=canaryPool
          - task: AzureDiagnostics@1
            inputs:
              query: "Heartbeat | where TimeGenerated > ago(1d)"
          - script: |
              echo 'Wait for 24h to validate metrics'
        # conditions for promotion/rollback are implemented in subsequent stages
  - stage: PromoteOrRollback
    dependsOn: CanaryUpgrade
    jobs:
      - job: Evaluate
        steps:
          - script: |
              if [ "$(az monitor metrics retrieve --resource /subscriptions/.../redis.canary --metric latency --aggregation Average)" -lt 100 ]; then
                echo "PROMOTE"
              else
                echo "ROLLBACK"
              fi
        - script: |
            echo 'Execute promotion or rollback actions and audit'
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you handle cross-region latency variability and data-residency constraints in this flow?
- Which metrics and thresholds would you monitor to minimize false positives and ensure user impact stays within SLOs?

questions.answer: Implement a YAML-driven Azure DevOps pipeline that pushes updated manifests to a dedicated gitops-repo, triggers FluxCD to reconcile changes, provisions per-tenant namespaces, retrieves per-environment secrets from Azure Key Vault, enforces policies using Kyverno/OPA frameworks, performs progressive canary rollouts via Istio (e.g., 5/95 traffic split), gates production deployments with approval stages, runs post-deployment drift detection against repository state, automatically rolls back by reverting gitops commits, and logs all pipeline steps to Azure Monitor Logs for complete observability.

questions.question: Design a beginner Azure DevOps YAML pipeline that builds a .NET API stored in a private repo, publishes a Docker image to Azure Container Registry, and deploys to Azure App Service using a Docker container with deployment slots for staging and production. Integrate per-environment settings from Azure App Configuration and secrets from Key Vault; gate production with a simple health-check script and automatically rollback by swapping slots if health checks fail. Include per-step logging to Azure Monitor?
questions.answer: Build a two-stage YAML pipeline with staging and production environments. The pipeline builds the .NET API, publishes a Docker image to Azure Container Registry, and deploys to Azure App Service using a staging slot. Retrieve per-environment settings from Azure App Configuration and secrets from Key Vault. Gate production deployment with health checks and implement automatic rollback by swapping slots if health checks fail. Include comprehensive logging to Azure Monitor for each step.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This tests the ability to design a practical, beginner-friendly pipeline that combines containerized deployment, per-environment configuration, secret management, health-based rollback, and monitoring—essential skills for modern DevOps practices.

## Key Concepts
- Azure DevOps YAML pipelines
- Docker containerization with Azure Container Registry
- Azure App Service deployment slots for safe releases
- Azure App Configuration and Key Vault integration
- Health checks and slot swap rollback mechanisms
- Azure Monitor and Log Analytics logging

## Code Example
```yaml
trigger:
- main

stages:
- stage: Staging
  jobs:
  - job: BuildAndDeploy
    steps:
      - script: echo "Building and deploying to staging..."
        displayName: 'Build and Deploy to Staging'
```

This skeleton structure demonstrates the foundation for a robust deployment pipeline that can be expanded with specific tasks for each requirement.
questions.diagram:
flowchart TD

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This question tests real-world, large-scale progressive delivery across many services with multi-region routing, a unified feature-flag source, and strong auditability. It emphasizes rollback safety, policy gates, and telemetry-driven validation.

## Key Concepts
- Progressive delivery across 20 microservices and multiple regions
- Front Door traffic shaping at per-service granularity
- Feature flags from Azure App Configuration and per-env secrets from Key Vault
- Azure Policy approvals as gates; canary and rollback logic
- Synthetic tests and Log Analytics drift validation for post-deploy safety
- Auditability of every step to Log Analytics

## Code Example
```yaml
# Partial pipeline sketch (high level)
trigger:
- main

stages:
- stage: CanaryRollout
  jobs:
  - job: DeployService
    steps:
    - task: AzureKeyVault@5
      inputs:
        azureSubscription: 'prod'
        KeyVaultName: 'kv-$(service)'
    - task: AzureRmWebAppDeployment@4
      inputs:
        ConnectionType: 'AzureRM'
        AppType: 'webapp'
        WebAppName: 'svc-$(service)'
        Package: '$(Pipeline.Workspace)/drop/$(service).zip'
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you test rollback safety when multiple services simultaneously roll back?
- How do you ensure audit log integrity across regions and services during replays?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Assesses practical, end-to-end migration planning for a multitenant SaaS on Azure with zero downtime, tenant isolation, backups, rollback readiness, and observability.

## Key Concepts

- Zero-downtime migrations and canary rollout at tenant granularity
- Liquibase integration for schema changes
- Per-tenant backups and rollback scripting
- Approvals, auditing, and Azure Monitor logs

## Code Example

```yaml
# Minimal pipeline snippet illustrating a canary migration stage
stages:
  - stage: CanaryMigration
    jobs:
      - job: MigrateTenant
        steps:
          - script: |
              # Execute Liquibase migration with tenant-specific parameters
              liquibase --changeLogFile=tenant-migrations.xml --url="$(TENANT_DB_URL)" update
            displayName: 'Execute Liquibase Migration'
          - task: AzureBackup@1
            inputs:
              connectedServiceName: '$(AZURE_CONNECTION)'
              resourceGroupName: '$(RESOURCE_GROUP)'
              location: '$(LOCATION)'
            displayName: 'Create Pre-Migration Backup'
```

## Implementation Approach

Structure pipeline stages sequentially: validation → canary migration (5% tenants) → automated testing → progressive rollout (25%, 50%, 100%) → final validation. Include manual approval gates between each rollout stage with rollback triggers on validation failures or performance degradation.

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Assesses practical Azure DevOps skills, blue/green deployment, per-environment secrets/flags, and safe rollback in a beginner-friendly scenario.

## Key Concepts

- YAML multi-stage pipelines
- Helm deployments in AKS with namespace isolation
- Azure App Configuration feature flags per environment
- Azure Key Vault secrets integration
- Traffic-switching and rollback safety
- Monitoring/logging with Azure Monitor

## Code Example

```yaml
# skeleton
trigger:
  branches:
    include:
    - main

stages:
- stage: Build
  jobs:
  - job: Build
    steps:
    - task: NodeTool@0
      inputs:
        versionSpec: '18.x'
    - script: npm install
    - script: npm test
- stage: Publish
  jobs:
  - job: BuildAndPush
    steps:
    - script: docker build -t <acr>/api:$(Build.BuildId) .
    - script: docker push <acr>/api:$(Build.BuildId)
- stage: DeployBlue
  jobs:
  - job: DeployBlue
    steps:
    - script: helm upgrade --install api-blue charts/api --namespace blue --set image.tag=$(Build.BuildId)
- stage: SmokeTest
  jobs:
  - job: Smoke
    steps:
    - script: ./scripts/smoke-test.sh
- stage: Promote
  dependsOn: SmokeTest
  condition: succeeded()
  jobs:
  - job: Promote
    steps:
    - task: ManualValidation@0
      inputs:
        instructions: 'Approve prod swap if blue is healthy'
- stage: Rollback
  dependsOn: Promote
  condition: failed()
  jobs:
  - job: Rollback
    steps:
    - script: helm upgrade --install api-green charts/api --namespace green --set image.tag=$(Build.BuildId)
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you handle secret rotation in this setup?
- How would you test the blue/green rollback in a CI environment?

Production deployments must require policy approvals, include post-deployment drift checks with automatic rollback capabilities, and log all steps to Azure Monitor for comprehensive observability. questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

This question evaluates expertise in enterprise-grade deployment architectures, testing proficiency with multi-region Kubernetes operations, secure secret management, dynamic feature flagging, service mesh routing, governance controls, and automated recovery mechanisms—capabilities essential for production systems at scale.

## Key Concepts

- **Multi-region AKS deployments** with environment isolation through region-scoped namespaces
- **Secure configuration management** using Azure Key Vault for secrets and Azure App Configuration for feature flags
- **Service mesh orchestration** with Istio enabling cross-cluster routing and progressive traffic shifting
- **Governance and compliance** through policy-based approval gates and automated drift detection
- **Operational resilience** with automatic rollback capabilities and centralized monitoring via Azure Monitor

## Code Example

```yaml
# Azure DevOps pipeline skeleton (high-level)
trigger:
  branches:
    include:
      - main

variables:
  - group: 'production-config'
  - name: 'imageRepository'
    value: 'mycontainer/microservice'

stages:
- stage: Build
  jobs:
  - job: BuildImage
    steps:
    - task: Docker@2
      inputs:
        containerRegistry: 'acr-service-connection'
        repository: $(imageRepository)
        command: 'buildAndPush'
        Dockerfile: 'Dockerfile'
        tags: |
          $(Build.BuildId)
          latest

- stage: DeployDev
  dependsOn: Build
  jobs:
  - deployment: DeployToDev
    environment: 'dev'
    strategy:
      runOnce:
        deploy:
          steps:
          - task: KubernetesManifest@0
            inputs:
              action: 'deploy'
              kubernetesServiceConnection: 'dev-aks'
              namespace: 'dev-region1'
              manifests: 'k8s/*.yaml'
              containers: '$(imageRepository):$(Build.BuildId)'
```

*Note: This skeleton demonstrates the core structure. Complete implementation would include additional stages for staging/production, policy gates, secret injection, Istio configuration, and monitoring integration.*

questions.question: Design an Azure DevOps YAML pipeline that, on PR against main in a microservices monorepo, builds and containerizes only touched services, deploys them to a per-PR ephemeral namespace in AKS, sources per-PR secrets from Azure Key Vault, enforces policy gates with Kyverno, runs end-to-end tests against a synthetic staging DB, and tears down the environment after PR close or TTL expiry, with logs in Azure Monitor and cost caps. What would your pipeline look like?
questions.answer: Implement a PR-driven workflow that detects changed services via path analysis, builds and containerizes only those services with PR-specific tags, pushes to Azure Container Registry, generates per-PR Helm values, deploys to an ephemeral AKS namespace, injects secrets from Azure Key Vault using managed identities, enforces Kyverno policy gates, runs comprehensive end-to-end tests against a synthetic staging database, and automatically tears down the environment when the PR closes or TTL expires.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This evaluates expertise in designing production-grade, GitOps-driven CI/CD pipelines that create isolated, ephemeral environments for pull requests while maintaining security, compliance, and cost efficiency.

## Key Concepts
- Selective builds based on path detection in monorepos
- PR-scoped container tagging and Azure Container Registry integration
- Managed identity authentication with Azure Key Vault
- Policy enforcement through Kyverno validation gates
- Ephemeral environments with automatic cleanup and TTL management
- GitOps reconciliation and drift prevention using FluxCD
- End-to-end testing with isolated staging databases
- Comprehensive observability via Azure Monitor with cost controls

## Code Example
```yaml
# azure-pipelines.yml - PR-driven ephemeral deployment pipeline
trigger:
  none
pr:
  branches:
    include:
    - main

variables:
  - group: azure-infrastructure
  - name: prNumber
    value: $(System.PullRequest.PullRequestNumber)
  - name: prTag
    value: 'pr-$(prNumber)'

stages:
- stage: DetectChanges
  displayName: 'Detect Changed Services'
  jobs:
  - job: detect
    steps:
    - bash: |
        git diff origin/main --name-only > changed_files.txt
        echo "##vso[task.setvariable variable=changedServices;isOutput=true]$(python detect-services.py changed_files.txt)"
      name: detect

- stage: BuildAndPush
  displayName: 'Build and Push Services'
  dependsOn: DetectChanges
  condition: succeeded()
  jobs:
  - job: build
    strategy:
      matrix: $[ stageDependencies.DetectChanges.detect.outputs['detect.changedServices'] ]
    steps:
    - task: Docker@2
      inputs:
        containerRegistry: $(acrServiceConnection)
        repository: $(service.name)
        tags: |
          $(prTag)
          $(Build.BuildNumber)
        command: buildAndPush
        Dockerfile: $(service.path)/Dockerfile
```

questions.question: Design an Azure DevOps YAML pipeline for a multi-service platform where every container image must pass security/compliance gates before deployment. Build the image, scan CVEs with Trivy, run license checks, generate a CycloneDX SBOM, sign artifacts with a Key Vault–backed key, and store the signature. Gate production with approvals; deploy canary to AKS; monitor with Azure Monitor and auto-rollback on gate failure?
questions.answer: Implement a pipeline that builds images, runs Trivy for CVE scanning, performs license checks, generates a CycloneDX SBOM, signs artifacts with a Key Vault–backed key, and stores the signature. Gate p
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
The question tests practical experience wiring security gates into CI/CD, artifact signing, and progressive deployment with observable outcomes.

## Key Concepts
- Security gates: CVE scanning, license checks, SBOM generation
- Artifact signing: using Azure Key Vault keys to sign images
- Approval gates: granular per-environment controls
- Canary deployments: AKS canary strategy with rollback on failure
- Observability: Azure Monitor integration for gate outcomes

## Code Example
```yaml
trigger:
- main

stages:
- stage: Build
  jobs:
  - job: build
    steps:
    - script: echo Build image
    - task: Docker@2
      inputs:
        command: buildAndPush
        repository: myrepo/myservice
        containerRegistry: myACR
        tags: $(Build.BuildId)
- stage: Gate
  dependsOn: Build
  jobs:
  - job: gate
    steps:
    - task: TrivyScan@1
      inputs:
        imageName: 'myrepo/myservice:$(Build.BuildId)'
    - script: |
        cyclonedx-bom -o sbom.json myrepo/myservice:$(Build.BuildId)
    - task: AzureKeyVault@2
      inputs:
        connectedServiceName: 'AZDO-SC'
        keyVaultName: 'kv-keys'
        secretsFilter: 'SIGNING_KEY'
    - script: |
        echo Signing artifact with KEY from Key Vault
    - task: ManualValidation@0
      inputs:
        timeout: '00:15:00'
- stage: Deploy
  dependsOn: Gate
  jobs:
  - deployment: Canary
    displayName: Deploy Canary to AKS
    environment: canary
    strategy:
      canary:
        steps:
        - script: echo Deploying canary
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you enforce per-tenant isolation in this flow?
- How would you automate rollback when a security gate fails in production?

questions.question: In Azure DevOps, design an end-to-end pattern to provide per-PR ephemeral environments in AKS for a microservices app. Use a single AKS cluster with namespace-per-PR, Helm for deployments, Azure Key Vault for per-PR secrets, and a TTL-based teardown. Describe the pipeline steps, gating, security, costs, and how to isolate test data and telemetry. End with ?
questions.answer: Create a unique namespace for each PR (e.g., pr-123) in your shared AKS cluster using kubectl create namespace. Deploy microservices using Helm with --namespace pr-$PR_NUMBER. Use AzureKeyVault@2 task to fetch secrets and Secrets Store CSI Driver for pod injection. Run integration tests with isolated test data (prefixed DB names). Delete namespace and Helm release on PR close or after 24-48 hours TTL via scheduled pipeline.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Question Matters
This tests your ability to build ephemeral environments for PR reviews - a key DevOps skill. You need to show you understand AKS namespaces, Helm deployments, secret management, and cost control through automated cleanup.

## Architecture Overview

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Azure DevOps Pipeline                        │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  1. Trigger: PR created/updated                                 │
│  2. Create Namespace (pr-{PR_ID})                               │
│  3. Deploy Helm charts to namespace                             │
│  4. Inject Key Vault secrets                                    │
│  5. Run integration tests                                      │
│  6. Update PR with environment URL                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Complete Pipeline YAML

```yaml
trigger: none
pr:
  branches:
    include:
      - '*'

variables:
  aksClusterName: 'ephemeral-aks-cluster'
  resourceGroup: 'rg-ephemeral-environments'
  helmVersion: '3.14.0'
  prNamespace: 'pr-$(System.PullRequest.PullRequestNumber)'
  keyVaultName: 'kv-ephemeral-secrets'

stages:
  - stage: Setup
    displayName: 'Create PR Environment'
    jobs:
      - job: CreateNamespace
        pool:
          vmImage: 'ubuntu-latest'
        steps:
          - task: AzureCLI@2
            displayName: 'Connect to AKS'
            inputs:
              azureSubscription: 'azure-service-connection'
              scriptType: 'bash'
              scriptLocation: 'inlineScript'
              inlineScript: |
                az aks get-credentials --name $(aksClusterName) --resource-group $(resourceGroup) --overwrite-existing

          - task: AzureCLI@2
            displayName: 'Create PR Namespace with Quotas'
            inputs:
              azureSubscription: 'azure-service-connection'
              scriptType: 'bash'
              scriptLocation: 'inlineScript'
              inlineScript: |
                kubectl create namespace $(prNamespace) --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -
                cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
                apiVersion: v1
                kind: ResourceQuota
                metadata:
                  name: pr-quota
                  namespace: $(prNamespace)
                spec:
                  hard:
                    requests.cpu: '4'
                    requests.memory: 8Gi
                    pods: '20'
                ---
                apiVersion: v1
                kind: LimitRange
                metadata:
                  name: pr-limits
                  namespace: $(prNamespace)
                spec:
                  limits:
                    - default:
                        cpu: '1'
                        memory: 2Gi
                      defaultRequest:
                        cpu: '100m'
                        memory: 256Mi
                      type: Container
                EOF

          - task: AzureKeyVault@2
            displayName: 'Fetch PR Secrets'
            inputs:
              azureSubscription: 'azure-service-connection'
              KeyVaultName: $(keyVaultName)
              SecretsFilter: '*'
              RunAsPreJob: false

  - stage: Deploy
    displayName: 'Deploy to PR Environment'
    dependsOn: Setup
    jobs:
      - job: HelmDeploy
        pool:
          vmImage: 'ubuntu-latest'
        steps:
          - task: AzureCLI@2
            displayName: 'Configure kubectl'
            inputs:
              azureSubscription: 'azure-service-connection'
              scriptType: 'bash'
              scriptLocation: 'inlineScript'
              inlineScript: |
                az aks get-credentials --name $(aksClusterName) --resource-group $(resourceGroup)

          - task: HelmInstaller@1
            displayName: 'Install Helm'
            inputs:
              helmVersionToInstall: $(helmVersion)

          - task: AzureCLI@2
            displayName: 'Deploy Helm Release'
            inputs:
              azureSubscription: 'azure-service-connection'
              scriptType: 'bash'
              scriptLocation: 'inlineScript'
              inlineScript: |
                helm upgrade --install pr-$(System.PullRequest.PullRequestNumber) ./charts/microservices 
                  --namespace $(prNamespace) --create-namespace 
                  --set image.tag=$(Build.BuildId) 
                  --set environment=pr-$(System.PullRequest.PullRequestNumber) 
                  --wait --timeout 5m

          - task: AzureCLI@2
            displayName: 'Create CSI SecretProviderClass'
            inputs:
              azureSubscription: 'azure-service-connection'
              scriptType: 'bash'
              scriptLocation: 'inlineScript'
              inlineScript: |
                cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
                apiVersion: secrets-store.csi.x-k8s.io/v1
                kind: SecretProviderClass
                metadata:
                  name: pr-azure-kv-secret
                  namespace: $(prNamespace)
                spec:
                  provider: azure
                  parameters:
                    useVMManagedIdentity: "true"
                    vaultName: "$(keyVaultName)"
                    secrets: |
                      - secretName: db-connection-string
                        objectName: "pr-$(System.PullRequest.PullRequestNumber)-db-connection"
                        objectType: secret
                EOF

  - stage: Test
    displayName: 'Run Integration Tests'
    dependsOn: Deploy
    jobs:
      - job: IntegrationTests
        pool:
          vmImage: 'ubuntu-latest'
        steps:
          - task: AzureCLI@2
            displayName: 'Run Isolated Tests'
            inputs:
              azureSubscription: 'azure-service-connection'
              scriptType: 'bash'
              scriptLocation: 'inlineScript'
              inlineScript: |
                export DB_NAME="test_pr$(System.PullRequest.PullRequestNumber)_orders"
                export TEST_USER_PREFIX="pr$(System.PullRequest.PullRequestNumber)_test_"
                npm test -- --testPathPattern=integration

  - stage: Teardown
    displayName: 'Cleanup PR Environment'
    condition: eq(variables['Build.Reason'], 'PullRequest')
    jobs:
      - job: CleanupResources
        pool:
          vmImage: 'ubuntu-latest'
        steps:
          - task: AzureCLI@2
            displayName: 'Delete Namespace & Helm Release'
            inputs:
              azureSubscription: 'azure-service-connection'
              scriptType: 'bash'
              scriptLocation: 'inlineScript'
              inlineScript: |
                az aks get-credentials --name $(aksClusterName) --resource-group $(resourceGroup) --overwrite-existing
                helm uninstall pr-$(System.PullRequest.PullRequestNumber) -n $(prNamespace) || true
                kubectl delete namespace $(prNamespace) --wait=true --timeout=5m || true
```

## TTL Cleanup Pipeline (Scheduled)

```yaml
trigger: none
schedules:
  - cron: '0 0 * * *'
    branches:
      include:
        - main

variables:
  aksClusterName: 'ephemeral-aks-cluster'
  resourceGroup: 'rg-ephemeral-environments'
  ttlHours: 48

pool:
  vmImage: 'ubuntu-latest'

steps:
  - task: AzureCLI@2
    displayName: 'Delete Expired PR Namespaces'
    inputs:
      azureSubscription: 'azure-service-connection'
      scriptType: 'bash'
      scriptLocation: 'inlineScript'
      inlineScript: |
        az aks get-credentials --name $(aksClusterName) --resource-group $(resourceGroup) --overwrite-existing
        for NS in $(kubectl get ns -o jsonpath='{range .items[?(@.metadata.name=~"^pr-")]} {.metadata.name} {end}'); do
          AGE_HOURS=$(($(date +%s) - $(date -d $(kubectl get ns $NS -o jsonpath='{.metadata.creationTimestamp}') +%s)) / 3600)
          if [ $AGE_HOURS -gt $(ttlHours) ]; then
            helm uninstall -n $NS || true
            kubectl delete ns $NS --wait=true
          fi
        done
```

## Security Considerations

| Area | Implementation |
|------|----------------|
| Network Isolation | Azure Network Policy restricts pod-to-pod communication between PR namespaces |
| Secret Isolation | Per-PR Key Vault secrets with restricted access policies; use Managed Identity |
| RBAC | Cluster-level RoleBinding to limit PR deployment access |
| Registry | Private endpoints for container registry |

## Cost Optimization

1. **Shared Cluster**: Single AKS cluster amortizes control plane costs
2. **Auto-Scaling**: Enable cluster autoscaler and HPA
3. **TTL Cleanup**: 24-48 hour TTL prevents orphaned resources
4. **Spot Nodes**: Use spot/preemptible VMs (~60-80% savings)
5. **Resource Quotas**: Prevent PR monopolizing cluster resources

## Test Data Isolation

- Database naming: `orders_pr123`, `users_pr123`
- Test user prefixes: `pr123_test_user_001`
- Message queue topics: `pr123_orders`

## Telemetry Isolation

- Use custom dimension `pr_id` in Application Insights to filter PR-specific telemetry
- Tag all log queries with `environment=pr-{PR_ID}`

  B --> C[Deploy Helm Release]
  C --> D[Run Integration Tests]
  D --> E[PR Closed or TTL Expired]
  E --> F[Teardown Namespace]

questions.answer: Configure a PR-only security gate for a Node.js service: trigger on PRs to main (trigger: none; pr: branches include: main) and skip pushes. Add a security job that runs npm ci, npm audit --json, and
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This tests if a junior Azure DevOps engineer can add a basic security gate to CI, understand PR gating, and use npm audit to block risky dependencies.

## Key Concepts
- PR triggers and gates in Azure Pipelines
- npm audit parsing and severity mapping
- Blocking PRs with pipeline failures and reporting results
- Minimal YAML and scripting knowledge

## Code Example
```javascript
trigger:
  none
pr:
  branches:
    include:
    - main

jobs:
- job: securityGate
  displayName: 'PR Security Gate'
  pool:
    vmImage: 'ubuntu-latest'
  steps:
  - script: npm ci
  - script: npm audit --json > npm-audit.json
  - script: |
      node -e "const d=require('./npm-audit.json'); const advisories=Object.values(d.advisories||{}); const high=advisories.filter(a=>a?.severity==='high' || a?.severity==='critical').length; if(high>0){ console.error('High/Critical advisories: '+high); process.exit(1); }" 
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you extend this to Yarn or Python (pip) projects?
- How would you surface details in the PR (comments, checks, or summary reports)?

questions.answer: The pipeline builds the Node.js API with npm ci, runs npm test, builds a Docker image and pushes to ACR, then deploys to a single AKS namespace with kubectl. It fetches per-environment settings from A

## Why This Is Asked
Tests understanding of end-to-end CD basics: source → build → test → containerize → push → deploy with kubectl; environment-specific config and secrets integration; security gates and rollback.

- Azure DevOps YAML pipelines
- Azure Container Registry (ACR)

- Post-deploy health checks and rollback
- Logging to Azure Monitor Logs

## Code Example
```yaml
trigger:
- main
pool:
  vmImage: 'ubuntu-latest'
steps:
- task: NodeTool@0
  inputs:
    versionSpec: '16.x'
- script: |
    npm ci
    npm test
  displayName: 'Install and test'
- task: Docker@2
  inputs:
    containerRegistry: '$(ACRServiceConnection)'
    repository: 'node-api'
    command: 'buildAndPush'
    Dockerfile: '**/Dockerfile'
    tags: |
      $(Build.BuildId)
- task: Kubernetes@1
  inputs:
    connectionType: 'Azure Resource Manager'
    azureSubscription: '$(AZ_SUBSCRIPTION)'
    azureResourceGroup: 'rg'
    kubernetesCluster: 'cluster'
    namespace: 'default'
    command: 'apply'
    useConfigurationFile: true
    configuration: |
      apiVersion: apps/v1
      kind: Deployment
      metadata:
        name: node-api
      spec:
        replicas: 1
        template:
          spec:
            containers:
            - name: node-api
              image: <ACR>/node-api:$(Build.BuildId)
              ports:
              - containerPort: 3000
```

questions.question: Design an intermediate Azure DevOps YAML pipeline for rotating per-tenant credentials and secrets for a SaaS app. Tenants are defined in a tenant.json. For each tenant, generate a short-lived Key Vault secret, update per-tenant App Configuration/connection strings, deploy a tenant-scoped canary to a separate AKS namespace, and gate promotions to prod with policy approvals. Validate drift after deployment and auto-rollback if drift detected; log all steps to Azure Monitor Logs?
questions.answer: Read tenant manifest, loop through tenants, rotate short-lived Key Vault secrets per tenant via Azure CLI, update per-tenant App Configuration and connection strings, deploy tenant-scoped canaries in dedicated AKS namespaces, gate production promotions with policy approvals, validate infrastructure drift post-deployment, trigger auto-rollback on drift detection, and log all operations to Azure Monitor Logs.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to drive secret lifecycle, per-tenant isolation, and policy-guarded promotions in a real-world multi-tenant SaaS setup. It also covers drift detection and rollback, which are critical for maintainable CI/CD.

## Key Concepts
- Per-tenant secret rotation with Azure Key Vault
- App Configuration and connection string management per tenant
- Tenant-scoped canary deployments in AKS
- Policy-based approvals for prod promotions
- Drift checks between IaC state and live resources
- Auto-rollback mechanisms and Azure Monitor logging

## Code Example
```yaml
trigger:
- main

pool:
  vmImage: 'ubuntu-latest'

variables:
  tenantConfig: 'tenant.json'
  keyVaultName: '$(KEY_VAULT_NAME)'
  aksCluster: '$(AKS_CLUSTER_NAME)'
  resourceGroup: '$(RESOURCE_GROUP)'

stages:
- stage: RotateSecrets
  displayName: 'Rotate Tenant Secrets'
  jobs:
  - job: ProcessTenants
    steps:
    - task: AzureCLI@2
      displayName: 'Read Tenant Manifest'
      inputs:
        azureSubscription: 'SERVICE_CONNECTION'
        scriptType: 'bash'
        scriptLocation: 'inlineScript'
        inlineScript: |
          tenants=$(cat $(tenantConfig) | jq -r '.tenants[].id')
          echo "##vso[task.setvariable variable=tenantList;isOutput=true]$tenants"
      name: tenantParser

    - ${{ each tenant in split(tenantParser.tenantList, ' ') }}:
      - task: AzureKeyVault@2
        displayName: 'Rotate Secret for ${{ tenant }}'
        inputs:
          azureSubscription: 'SERVICE_CONNECTION'
          KeyVaultName: '$(keyVaultName)'
          SecretsFilter: '${{ tenant }}-*'
          RunAsPreJob: false

      - task: AzureCLI@2
        displayName: 'Update App Configuration for ${{ tenant }}'
        inputs:
          azureSubscription: 'SERVICE_CONNECTION'
          scriptType: 'bash'
          scriptLocation: 'inlineScript'
          inlineScript: |
            az appconfig kv set --name ${{ tenant }}-appconfig --key "connection-string" --value $(NEW_SECRET_VALUE)

- stage: DeployCanary
  displayName: 'Deploy Tenant Canaries'
  dependsOn: RotateSecrets
  jobs:
  - job: DeployToAKS
    steps:
    - task: KubernetesManifest@1
      displayName: 'Deploy Canary to Namespace'
      inputs:
        action: 'deploy'
        kubernetesServiceConnection: 'K8S_CONNECTION'
        namespace: 'tenant-$(tenantId)-canary'
        manifests: 'k8s/canary-deployment.yaml'
        strategy: 'canary'
        percentage: '25'

- stage: ValidateAndPromote
  displayName: 'Validate Drift and Promote'
  dependsOn: DeployCanary
  jobs:
  - job: DriftDetection
    steps:
    - task: AzureCLI@2
      displayName: 'Check Infrastructure Drift'
      inputs:
        azureSubscription: 'SERVICE_CONNECTION'
        scriptType: 'bash'
        scriptLocation: 'inlineScript'
        inlineScript: |
          az deployment group validate --resource-group $(resourceGroup) --template-file infra/template.json
          if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
            echo "Drift detected - initiating rollback"
            az deployment group create --resource-group $(resourceGroup) --template-file infra/rollback.json
            exit 1
          fi

    - task: AzureMonitorLogs@0
      displayName: 'Log Deployment Status'
      inputs:
        azureSubscription: 'SERVICE_CONNECTION'
        logType: 'CustomLog'
        workspaceName: 'LOG_ANALYTICS_WORKSPACE'
        logData: '{"operation": "deployment", "status": "success", "tenant": "$(tenantId)", "timestamp": "$(date +%s)"}'

  - deployment: PromoteToProd
    displayName: 'Promote to Production'
    environment: 'production'
    strategy:
      runOnce:
        deploy:
          steps:
          - task: KubernetesManifest@1
            displayName: 'Promote Canary to Production'
            inputs:
              action: 'deploy'
              kubernetesServiceConnection: 'K8S_CONNECTION'
              namespace: 'tenant-$(tenantId)-prod'
              manifests: 'k8s/production-deployment.yaml'
```

questions.question: Design an Azure DevOps YAML pipeline to deploy a multi-tenant API to two Azure regions with data residency constraints. Each tenant uses its own AKS namespace in both regions; pull per-tenant config from App Configuration and secrets from Key Vault; route region-aware traffic via Front Door with latency budgets and a 10/90 canary to the secondary region after cross-region synthetic tests pass; gate prod with policy approvals; implement drift checks against the manifest repo and auto-rollback; log to Azure Monitor?
questions.answer: Propose a YAML with a tenant matrix, creating AKS namespaces in both regions, binding per-tenant App Configuration values and Key Vault secrets, deploying Helm charts to both regions, Front Door handl
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests handling of cross-region, multi-tenant deployments under data residency constraints, plus governance, testing, and observability in a real DevOps setup.

## Key Concepts
- Multi-region AKS deployments and per-tenant namespaces
- Azure App Configuration for per-tenant settings
- Azure Key Vault for per-tenant secrets
- Front Door for geo-aware traffic routing with latency budgets
- Canary deployment strategy (10/90) to secondary region
- Policy gates (e.g., Kyverno/OPA) and approvals
- Drift checks comparing desired vs actual manifests
- Auto-rollback on failures and drift
- Centralized logging to Azure Monitor

## Code Example
```yaml
# Skeleton YAML illustrating tenant matrix and multi-region deploy
trigger:
  branches:
    include:
    - main
stages:
  - stage: Deploy
    jobs:
      - job: DeployGeo
        steps:
          - script: echo Deploy per-tenant namespaces in both regions
            displayName: CreateNamespaces
          - script: echo Bind App Configuration and Key Vault per tenant
            displayName: BindSecretsAndConfig
          - script: echo Deploy Helm charts to both regions
            displayName: DeployHelm
          - script: echo Run synthetic tests in both regions
            displayName: RunTests
          - script: echo Apply Front Door routing with 10/90 canary
            displayName: CanaryTraffic
          - script: echo Gate approvals and drift checks
            displayName: GateAndDrift
          - script: echo Auto-rollback if issues detected

```

questions.question: In Azure DevOps for a monorepo with multiple npm packages, design a PR validation that runs tests only for changed packages, builds per-package artifacts, and gates PR merge until all relevant tests pass; include a minimal YAML snippet showing path-based triggers, npm workspaces, and artifact publishing?
questions.answer: Use Azure DevOps path filters with changed files detection and npm workspaces for selective testing. Implement a dynamic matrix that only includes packages with changes, then run tests and publish artifacts per package.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Monorepo PR validation must balance speed with coverage by testing only affected packages while maintaining quality gates.

## Key Concepts
- Path-based triggers and changed files detection
- npm workspaces and selective package testing
- Dynamic matrix strategy and conditional job execution
- Per-package artifact publishing

## Code Example

```yaml
# azure-pipelines.yml
trigger:
  none
pr:
  branches:
    include:
    - '*'

variables:
  - name: changedPackages
    value: ''
  - name: workspaceRoot
    value: '$(Build.SourcesDirectory)'

stages:
- stage: DetectChanges
  jobs:
  - job: GetChangedPackages
    steps:
    - task: NodeTool@0
      inputs:
        versionSpec: '18.x'
    - script: |
        # Get changed files and extract package names
        changedFiles=$(git diff --name-only HEAD~1 HEAD)
        packages=$(echo "$changedFiles" | grep -E '^packages/[^/]+/' | cut -d'/' -f2 | sort -u | tr '\n' ' ')
        echo "##vso[task.setvariable variable=changedPackages;isOutput=true]$packages"
      name: detectChanges
    - script: |
        echo "Changed packages: $(detectChanges.changedPackages)"

- stage: TestAndBuild
  dependsOn: DetectChanges
  condition: ne(dependencies.DetectChanges.outputs['GetChangedPackages.detectChanges.changedPackages'], '')
  jobs:
  - job: TestPackages
    strategy:
      matrix:
        $[ each(package in split(dependencies.DetectChanges.outputs['GetChangedPackages.detectChanges.changedPackages'], ' ')) ]:
          ${{ package }}:
            packageName: ${{ package }}
    steps:
    - task: NodeTool@0
      inputs:
        versionSpec: '18.x'
    - script: |
        # Install dependencies for the specific package
        npm ci --workspace=@myorg/$(packageName)
      displayName: 'Install package dependencies'
    - script: |
        # Run tests for the specific package
        npm test --workspace=@myorg/$(packageName)
      displayName: 'Run package tests'
    - script: |
        # Build the package
        npm run build --workspace=@myorg/$(packageName)
      displayName: 'Build package'
    - task: PublishBuildArtifacts@1
      inputs:
        PathtoPublish: 'packages/$(packageName)/dist'
        ArtifactName: '$(packageName)-artifact'
      displayName: 'Publish package artifact'
      condition: succeeded()

- stage: QualityGate
  dependsOn: TestAndBuild
  jobs:
  - job: ValidateAllTests
    steps:
    - script: |
        echo "All changed packages have been tested and built successfully"
        echo "PR validation passed - ready for merge"
      displayName: 'Quality gate validation'
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you handle inter-package dependencies in the monorepo?
- What strategies would you use for caching npm dependencies to speed up builds?
- How would you implement test result reporting and coverage thresholds?
questions.diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[PR Opened] --> B[Detect touched packages via paths]
  B --> C[Run per-package matrix tests]
  C --> D[Publish per-package artifacts]
  D --> E[Gate merge on success]

questions.answer: Use a matrix to test Node 14 and 16 and Cache@2 to persist npm cache between PR builds. Key should include package-lock.json so changes invalidate the cache. Example YAML: (refer to explanation for fu
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests knowledge of Azure Pipelines matrix builds, caching strategy, and cache invalidation tied to lockfiles—crucial for reliable PR speed.

## Key Concepts

- Azure Pipelines matrix strategy for Node versions
- Cache@2 task and cache key composition
- npm ci vs npm install in CI
- Cache invalidation via package-lock.json

## Code Example

```yaml
trigger:
- '**/*'
pr:
- '*'
pool:
  vmImage: ubuntu-latest

variables:
  npmCache: '$(Pipeline.Workspace)/.npm'

strategy:
  matrix:
    Node14:
      NodeVersion: '14'
    Node16:
      NodeVersion: '16'

steps:
- task: UseNode@1
  inputs:
    versionSpec: '$(NodeVersion)'
  displayName: 'Use Node.js $(NodeVersion)'

- task: Cache@2
  inputs:
    key: 'npm | $(Agent.OS) | package-lock.json'
    restoreKeys: |
      npm | $(Agent.OS)
    path: '~/.npm'
  displayName: 'Cache npm'

- script: npm ci
  displayName: 'Install dependencies'

- script: npm test
  displayName: 'Run tests'
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you invalidate the cache if you update a monorepo workspace layout?
- What additional steps would you add to ensure deterministic builds across agents?

questions.question: Design an advanced Azure DevOps YAML pipeline to deploy a multi-service app to AKS. Each service has its own Helm chart and namespace; a separate GitOps repo (Argo CD) reconciles. Secrets come from Key Vault; per-environment config from App Configuration. Implement per-service canaries, drift checks vs repo, policy gates (Azure Policy + OPA), SBOM gates, and health checks to Azure Monitor. Prod requires approvals; auto-rollback on canary or drift?
questions.answer: I would implement a multi-stage Azure DevOps YAML pipeline. First, build and package each microservice's Helm chart, then push the charts to an OCI-compatible Helm repository. The pipeline would then trigger Argo CD to reconcile the changes into each service's dedicated namespace within the AKS cluster. Secrets would be securely retrieved from Azure Key Vault using a Managed Identity, while per-environment configuration would be sourced from Azure App Configuration. For progressive delivery, I would implement per-service canary deployments using Argo CD's analysis and rollout capabilities. The pipeline would also perform drift checks against the GitOps repository to detect unauthorized changes, enforce policy gates using Azure Policy and OPA, generate Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) for each service, and forward health checks and logs to Azure Monitor for observability. Production deployments would require manual approvals, and the system would automatically rollback on canary failures or significant drift detected.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This question evaluates end-to-end experience with Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) deployments, GitOps workflows, and policy-driven governance in enterprise environments.

## Key Concepts
- **AKS & Helm**: Multi-service architecture with isolated namespaces per service
- **GitOps**: Argo CD reconciliation for declarative infrastructure
- **Security**: Azure Key Vault secrets with Managed Identity for authentication
- **Configuration**: Azure App Configuration for environment-specific values
- **Progressive Delivery**: Canary deployments, drift detection, and automated rollback
- **Governance**: Azure Policy and OPA for policy enforcement
- **Compliance**: SBOM generation for supply chain security
- **Observability**: Azure Monitor for health checks and logging
- **Change Management**: Manual approvals and auto-rollback strategies

## Code Example
```yaml
# Azure DevOps Pipeline - Multi-Service AKS Deployment
trigger:
  - main

variables:
  helmRepo: 'oci://acr.azurecr.io/helm-charts'
  aksCluster: 'aks-production'
  rg: 'rg-aks-prod'

stages:
  - stage: Build
    displayName: 'Build & Package Services'
    jobs:
      - job: BuildServices
        steps:
          - task: Docker@4
            inputs:
              containerRegistry: 'acrConnection'
              repository: '$(serviceName)'
              command: 'buildAndPush'
          - task: HelmDeploy@0
            inputs:
              command: 'package'
              chartPath: 'charts/$(serviceName)'
              version: '$(Build.BuildNumber)'
```

questions.question: Design an advanced Azure DevOps YAML pipeline for a multi-tenant SaaS app deployed to two AKS clusters across regions. The pipeline must: build and push images, run unit/integration tests, generate an SBOM, and publish images to ACR. It must implement per-tenant configuration from Azure App Configuration, per-tenant secrets from Key Vault via managed identity, and a GitOps workflow with FluxCD using a separate gitops-repo. Include a progressive canary rollout per tenant with Istio, require regional production approvals, auto-rollback on drift, and centralized logging to Azure Monitor Logs. Provide a concrete pipeline skeleton and explain handling of secret rotation and drift detection?
questions.answer: I'd implement a two-repo GitOps model with a dedicated gitops-repo for FluxCD per-tenant manifests and a single Azure DevOps YAML pipeline that builds, tests, SBOM-scans, and pushes images to ACR. Per-tenant configuration flows from Azure App Configuration via Istio's mesh expansion, while per-tenant secrets are injected from Azure Key Vault using managed identity and the CSI Secret Store provider. The pipeline triggers FluxCD reconciliation via a GitHub/GitLab commit, which then performs progressive canary rollouts per tenant using Istio traffic splitting with regional production approval gates. Auto-rollback occurs when FluxCD detects drift between desired state (git) and actual state (cluster), leveraging Azure Monitor Logs for centralized observability and alerting.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This question tests the ability to design complex, real-world CI/CD for multi-tenant, region-distributed SaaS applications with GitOps, secret management, and drift handling.

## Key Concepts
- Multi-tenant GitOps repositories with FluxCD per-tenant manifests
- Per-tenant configuration from Azure App Configuration via CSI provider
- Secrets via Azure Key Vault with managed identity (no static credentials)
- Progressive canary rollout across regions using Istio traffic shifting
- Drift detection through FluxCD reconciliation and auto-rollback
- Centralized logging to Azure Monitor Logs (Log Analytics)

## Code Example
```yaml
# Azure DevOps YAML Pipeline - Multi-Tenant AKS GitOps Flow
trigger: none
pr: none

resources:
  repositories:
  - repository: gitops
    type: git
    name: acr-prod/gitops-repo
    ref: main

stages:
- stage: Build
  jobs:
  - job: BuildAndTest
    steps:
    - script: az acr build -r $(acrName) -t $(imageName):$(Build.BuildId) .
      displayName: Build and push to ACR
    - script: npm test -- --coverage
      displayName: Unit & Integration Tests
    - task: Syft@0
      inputs:
        image: $(acrName).azurecr.io/$(imageName):$(Build.BuildId)
        output: cyclonedx-json
        file: sbom.json
    - task: PublishPipelineArtifact@1
      inputs:
        targetPath: sbom.json
        artifact: sbom

- stage: DeployToStaging
  dependsOn: Build
  jobs:
  - deployment: DeployStaging
    environment: staging
    strategy:
      runOnce:
        deploy:
          steps:
          - script: |
              cd gitops-repo/clusters/$(location)/staging
              git config user.email "devops@company.com"
              git config user.name "Azure Pipelines"
              cat > values.yaml << EOF
              image:
                tag: "$(Build.BuildId)"
              tenantConfig:
                configStore: "appconfig-$(tenantId)"
              EOF
              git add . && git commit -m "Deploy $(Build.BuildId)" && git push
            displayName: Update GitOps repo

- stage: CanaryPerTenant
  dependsOn: DeployToStaging
  jobs:
  - job: ApplyIstioCanary
    steps:
    - task: Kubernetes@1
      displayName: Apply Istio VirtualService canary
      inputs:
        connectionType: Kubernetes Service Connection
        kubernetesServiceConnection: $(aksCluster)
        namespace: tenant-$(tenantId)
        command: apply
        useConfigurationFile: true
        configuration: canary-manifest.yaml

- stage: RegionalApproval
  dependsOn: CanaryPerTenant
  jobs:
  - job: WaitForApproval
    pool: server
    timeoutInMinutes: 4320
    steps:
    - task: ManualValidation@0
      timeoutInMinutes: 1440
      inputs:
        instructions: 'Approve production deployment to $(location)'
        onTimeout: reject

- stage: ProductionRollout
  dependsOn: RegionalApproval
  jobs:
  - deployment: DeployProduction
    environment: production
    strategy:
      runOnce:
        deploy:
          steps:
          - script: kubectl rollout status deployment -n tenant-$(tenantId)
            displayName: Verify rollout
```

## Secret Rotation & Drift Detection

### Secret Rotation
- Use Azure Key Vault with **automatic rotation** policies (time-based or event-based)
- Pods consume secrets via **Azure Key Vault CSI Provider**, which automatically watches for changes
- No pipeline changes needed when secrets rotate—pods remount automatically
- For Azure DevOps service connections, use **workload identity federation** instead of stored secrets

### Drift Detection
- **FluxCD** continuously reconciles git state with cluster state (default every 60 seconds)
- When drift is detected (manual changes, failed rollout, config mismatch), FluxCD marks the resource as `OutOfSync`
- **Alerting**: Configure Azure Monitor Logs to query FluxCD controllers for `level=error` events, triggering Azure Alerts
- **Auto-rollback**: Configure FluxCD with `spec.healthChecks` and `spec.timeout` to auto-heal or rollback on failure
- **Azure Monitor**: Stream FluxCD controller logs to Log Analytics workspace for centralized drift analysis

  F --> G[Approvals & Prod]
  G --> H[Azure Monitor Logs]

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Assess DR design, cross-region provisioning, test orchestration, metric collection, and failover control under real-world constraints.

## Key Concepts
- Cross-region ARM deployments and idempotent templates
- Synthetic load testing and end-to-end validation
- RTO/RPO measurement and Azure Monitor integration
- Approval gates and automated rollback
- Safe data seeding and tenant isolation

## Code Example
```yaml
trigger:
  branches:
    include:
      - main
stages:
- stage: DRProvision
  jobs:
  - job: DeployRegions
    steps:
      - task: AzureCLI@2
        inputs:
          scriptType: bash
          scriptLocation: inlineScript
          inlineScript: |
            az group create -n dr-rg-a -l eastus
            az group create -n dr-rg-b -l westus
- stage: DeployTests
  dependsOn: DRProvision
  jobs:
  - job: DeployRegionA
    steps:
      - script: echo Deploy to Region A
  - job: DeployRegionB
    steps:
      - script: echo Deploy to Region B
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you implement drift detection between the two DR environments? 
- What metrics and alerts would you configure to ensure SLA compliance and quick rollback?

Outline a per-tenant environment bootstrap, secret injection into deployments, governance checks (Azure Policy, approvals), telemetry tagging to prevent cross-tenant leakage, TTL-based teardown, and a robust rollback path with canary moves. How would you implement this end-to-end?
questions.answer: Use a tenant-driven YAML pipeline that iterates a tenants manifest to provision per-tenant environments (namespace/Resource Group), fetches secrets from each tenant's Key Vault via the AzureKeyVault t
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to design scalable, secure multi-tenant deployments with per-tenant secrets, governance, telemetry isolation, and robust rollback strategies in Azure DevOps.

## Key Concepts
- Tenant isolation and per-tenant Key Vaults
- Secret injection into deployments without leakage
- Policy checks and approvals as gates
- Telemetry tagging by tenantId and audit trails
- TTL-based teardown and canary rollback

## Code Example
```yaml
# example per-tenant deployment skeleton
trigger:
  none
variables:
  tenants: |
    - name: tenantA
      vault: tenantA-vault
      rg: tenantA-rg
    - name: tenantB
      vault: tenantB-vault
      rg: tenantB-rg
stages:
- stage: Deploy
  jobs:
  - job: DeployTenant
    strategy:
      matrix:
        tenantA:
          name: tenantA
          vault: tenantA-vault
          rg: tenantA-rg
        tenantB:
          name: tenantB
          vault: tenantB-vault
          rg: tenantB-rg
    steps:
    - task: AzureKeyVault@2
      inputs:
        connectedServiceName: 'az-span-conn'
        keyVaultName: $(vault)
        secretsFilter: '*'
    - script: echo Deploying to $(name)
      # deployment steps using Helm/ARM with secret values injected
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you enforce per-tenant data isolation in logs and telemetry?
- What failure modes are likely, and how would you validate the TTL teardown and rollback pathways?
- How would you scale this to thousands of tenants while keeping governance fast?
